title: Double integrals with variable bounds
description: Evaluate the given double integral between bound 1 and bound 2, which are functions, not numbers.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-1}^1 \int_0^{y^2} x^2 + y^2 \, dx \, dy =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $y^2$ at the end as if it were a numerical bound.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_{-1}^1 \int_0^{y^2} x^2 + y^2 \, dx \, dy &= \int_{-1}^1 \left[ \dfrac{x^3}{3} + xy^2 \right]_0^{y^2} \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_{-1}^1 \dfrac{y^6}{3} + y^4 \, dy
\end{align}$

Second, we evaluate the outer integral.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_{-1}^1 \dfrac{y^6}{3} + y^4 \, dy &= \left[ \dfrac{y^7}{21} + \dfrac{y^5}{5} \right]_{-1}^1 \\ \\
&= \dfrac{1}{21} + \dfrac{1}{5} - \left( \dfrac{-1}{21} + \dfrac{-1}{5} \right) \\ \\
&= \dfrac{2}{21} + \dfrac{2}{5} \\ \\
&= \dfrac{52}{105}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-1}^1 \int_0^{y^2} x^2 + y^2 \, dx \, dy = \dfrac{52}{105}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^2 \int_{-y}^y x - 4y \, dx \, dy =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $-y$ and $y$ at the end as if they were numerical bounds.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_{0}^2 \int_{-y}^y x - 4y \, dx \, dy &= \int_0^2 \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} - 4xy \right]_{-y}^y dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 \dfrac{y^2}{2} - 4y^2 - \left( \dfrac{y^2}{2}  + 4y^2 \right) \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 -8y^2 \, dy
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{0}^2 \int_{-y}^y x - 4y \, dx \, dy = \dfrac{-64}{3}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_0^\pi \int_0^{\sin(y)} 2x - \sin(y) \, dx \, dy =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $\sin(y)$ at the end as if it were a numerical bound.

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle \int_0^\pi \int_0^{\sin(y)} 2x - \sin(y) \, dx \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^\pi \left[ x^2 - x\sin(y) \right]_0^{\sin(y)} \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^\pi \sin^2(y) - \sin^2(y) \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^\pi 0 \, dy
\end{align}$

Second, we evaluate the outer integral, which is zero.

$\begin{align}
\int_0^\pi 0 \, dy = 0
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_0^\pi \int_0^{\sin(y)} 2x - \sin(y) \, dx \, dy = 0$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_1^{\sqrt{y}} x - 2y^2 \, dx \, dy =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $\sqrt{y}$ at the end as if it were a numerical bound.

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_1^{\sqrt{y}} x - 2y^2 \, dx \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 \left[ \dfrac{x^2}{2} - 2xy^2 \right]_1^{\sqrt{y}} dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 \left( \dfrac{y}{2} - 2y^{2.5} \right) - \left( \dfrac{1}{2} - 2y^2 \right) \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 \dfrac{y}{2} - 2y^{2.5} - \dfrac{1}{2} + 2y^2 \, dy
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_1^{\sqrt{y}} x - 2y^2 \, dx \, dy = \dfrac{16}{3} - \dfrac{32 \sqrt{2}}{7}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_1^3 \int_{-\sqrt{y}}^{\sqrt{y}} x^3 + 2 \, dx \, dy =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $-\sqrt{y}$ and $\sqrt{y}$ at the end as if they were numerical bounds.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_1^3 \int_{-\sqrt{y}}^{\sqrt{y}} x^3 + 2 \, dx \, dy &= \int_1^3 \left[ \dfrac{x^4}{4} + 2x \right]_{-\sqrt{y}}^{\sqrt{y}} dy \\ \\
&= \int_1^3 4\sqrt{y} \, dy
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_1^3 \int_{-\sqrt{y}}^{\sqrt{y}} x^3 + 2 \, dx \, dy = 8\sqrt{3} - \dfrac{8}{3}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_0^1 \int_{-y^2}^{0} 3x + 2y \, dx \, dy =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $-y^2$ at the end as if it were a numerical bound.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_0^1 \int_{-y^2}^{0} 3x + 2y \, dx \, dy &= \int_0^1 \left[ \dfrac{3x^2}{2} + 2xy \right]_{-y^2}^0 dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^1 -\left( \dfrac{3y^4}{2} - 2y^3 \right) \, dy \\ \\
&= \int_0^1 2y^3 - \dfrac{3y^4}{2} \, dy
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_0^1 \int_{-y^2}^{0} 3x + 2y \, dx \, dy = \dfrac{1}{5}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_0^{2\pi} \int_{\cos(x)}^{\cos(x) + 1} x + y \, dy \, dx =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $\cos(x)$ and $\cos(x) + 1$ at the end as if they were numerical bounds.

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle \int_0^{2\pi} \int_{\cos(x)}^{\cos(x) + 1} x + y \, dy \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} \left[ xy + \dfrac{y^2}{2} \right]_{\cos(x)}^{\cos(x) + 1} \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} x(\cos(x) + 1) + \dfrac{(\cos(x) + 1)^2}{2} \\ \\
&- \left( x\cos(x) + \dfrac{\cos^2(x)}{2} \right) \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi} x + \cos(x) + \dfrac{1}{2} \, dx
\end{align}$

Second, we evaluate the outer integral.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_0^{2\pi} x + \cos(x) + \dfrac{1}{2} \, dx &= \dfrac{x^2}{2} + \sin(x) + \dfrac{x}{2} \bigg|_0^{2\pi} \\ \\
&= \dfrac{4\pi^2}{2} + \sin(2\pi) + \dfrac{2\pi}{2} \\ \\
&- \left( \dfrac{0}{2} + \sin(0) + \dfrac{0}{2} \right) \\ \\
&= \pi + 2\pi^2
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_0^{2\pi} \int_{\cos(x)}^{\cos(x) + 1} x + y \, dy \, dx = \pi + 2\pi^2$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-\pi}^0 \int_{\sin(x)}^{2} 3 + \cos(x) \, dy \, dx =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $\sin(x)$ at the end as if it were a numerical bound.

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle \int_{-\pi}^0 \int_{\sin(x)}^{2} 3 + \cos(x) \, dy \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_{-\pi}^0 \left[ 3y + y\cos(x) \right]_{\sin(x)}^2 dx \\ \\
&= \int_{-\pi}^0 6 + 2\cos(x) - 3\sin(x) - \sin(x)\cos(x) \, dx
\end{align}$

Second, we evaluate the outer integral. We can use the trigonometric identity $\sin(2x) = 2\sin(x)\cos(x)$ to integrate $\sin(x)\cos(x)$.

$\begin{align}
&\int_{-\pi}^0 6 + 2\cos(x) - 3\sin(x) - \sin(x)\cos(x) \, dx \\ \\
&= \left[ 6x + 2\sin(x) + 3\cos(x) + \dfrac{1}{4} \cos(2x) \right]_{-\pi}^0 \\ \\
&= 3 (1) + \dfrac{1}{4} - \left( -6\pi + 3(-1) + \dfrac{1}{4} \right) \\ \\
&= 6\pi + 6
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-\pi}^0 \int_{\sin(x)}^{2} 3 + \cos(x) \, dy \, dx = 6\pi + 6$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_{-x}^{2x} 4x + 2y \, dy \, dx =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $-x$ and $2x$ at the end as if they were numerical bounds.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_{-x}^{2x} 4x + 2y \, dy \, dx &= \int_0^2 \left[ 4xy + y^2 \right]_{-x}^{2x} \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 8x^2 + 4x^2 - (-4x^2 + x^2) \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 15x^2 \, dx
\end{align}$

Second, we evaluate the outer integral.

$\begin{align}
\int_0^2 15x^2 \, dx &= \left[ 5x^3 \right])0^2 \\ \\
&= 40
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_{-x}^{2x} 4x + 2y \, dy \, dx = 40$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_{-1}^1 \int_0^{x^2} x + 3y \, dy \, dx =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $x^2$ at the end as if it were a numerical bound.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_{-1}^1 \int_0^{x^2} x + 3y \, dy \, dx &= \int_{-1}^1 \left[ xy + \dfrac{3y^2}{2} \right]_0^{x^2} \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_{-1}^1 x^3  + \dfrac{3x^4}{2} \, dx
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_{-1}^1 \int_0^{x^2} x + 3y \, dy \, dx = \dfrac{3}{5}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_{-2x}^{x^2} x - x^2 \, dy \, dx =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $-2x$ and $x^2$ at the end as if they were numerical bounds.

$\begin{align}
&\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_{-2x}^{x^2} x - x^2 \, dy \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 \left[ xy - x^2y \right]_{-2x}^{x^2} \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 \left(x^3 - x^4 \right) - \left( -2x^2 + 2x^3 \right) \, dx \\ \\
&= \int_0^2 -x^4 - x^3 + 2x^2 \, dx
\end{align}$

Second, we evaluate the outer integral.

$\begin{align}
\int_0^2 -x^4 - x^3 + 2x^2 \, dx &= \left[ \dfrac{-x^5}{5} - \dfrac{x^4}{4} + \dfrac{2x^3}{3} \right]_0^2 \\ \\
&= \dfrac{-32}{5} - \dfrac{16}{4} + \dfrac{16}{3} \\ \\
&= \dfrac{-384 - 240 + 320}{60} \\ \\
&= \dfrac{-304}{60} \\ \\
&= \dfrac{-76}{15}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_0^2 \int_{-2x}^{x^2} x - x^2 \, dy \, dx = \dfrac{-76}{15}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Evaluate the double integral.**

$\displaystyle \int_1^{\ln(2)} \int_0^{e^x} -2x + 2y \, dy \, dx =$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, we evaluate the inner integral. We can substitute in the $e^x$ at the end as if it were a numerical bound.

$\begin{align}
\displaystyle \int_1^{\ln(2)} \int_0^{e^x} -2x + 2y \, dy \, dx &= \int_1^{\ln(2)} \left[ -2xy + y^2 \right]_0^{e^x} dx \\ \\
&= \int_1^{\ln(2)} -2xe^x + (e^x)^2 dx \\ \\
&= \int_1^{\ln(2)} -2xe^x + e^{2x} dx
\end{align}$

Second, we evaluate the outer integral. We can use integration by parts to find that the integral of $-2xe^x \, dx$ is $2(x - 1)e^x$.

$\begin{align}
&\int_1^{\ln(2)} -2xe^x + e^{2x} dx \\ \\
&= \left[ -2(x - 1)e^x + \dfrac{e^{2x}}{2} \right]_1^{\ln(2)} \\ \\
&= -2 (\ln(2) - 1)e^{\ln(2)} + \dfrac{e^{2\ln(2)}}{2} - \left( \dfrac{e^2}{2} \right) \\ \\
&= -4(\ln(2) - 1) + \dfrac{e^{\ln(4)}}{2} - \dfrac{e^2}{2} \\ \\
&= 6 - 4\ln(2) - \dfrac{e^2}{2}
\end{align}$

The answer:

$\displaystyle \int_1^{\ln(2)} \int_0^{e^x} -2x + 2y \, dy \, dx = 6 - 4\ln(2) - \dfrac{e^2}{2}$
